nativeRange.habitat: Shallow outer reefs subject to surge; spur-and-groove zones with bare rock, rubble, and coral; depth range 0–20 m

diet.notes: Feeds on a varied diet including squid, krill, clams, small fish, and hard-shelled shrimp; hard shelled prey helps wear down constantly growing teeth. In the wild also consumes algae, mollusks, crustaceans, worms, echinoderms, sponges, and foraminiferans. LiveAquaria categorizes it as a carnivore in a captive-care context.

imageCandidates.notes: Photograph by Brian Gratwicke, originally posted to Flickr 2012-02-26. High resolution (3228 × 2198 px). Unambiguously identified as Rhinecanthus rectangulus on the Commons page.

Photo by J.E. Randall, Hawaii. FishBase states copyright remains with the photographer/institution; license not explicitly stated. Randall photos are generally considered reliable species identifications.
Product hero image from LiveAquaria species page. Clean, watermark-free retailer product photo. Recommended only as fallback if no CC-licensed image is suitable.

summary: Hawaii's state fish, the Humu Rectangle Triggerfish is a bold, colorful reef predator with striking black, white, and yellow markings. Hardy and easy to feed but aggressive — not reef safe.
careNotes:
The Humu Rectangle Triggerfish (Hawaii's state fish) is a hardy and entertaining species but is generally advised to be kept in a large tank — at least 180 gallons (~681 L) due to its size and territorial nature. It will rearrange rocks and decor, and have been known to vocalize using a grunting sound.

Not reef compatible — will generally eat invertebrates and disturb corals. Best kept with other large, semi-aggressive to aggressive fish of similar size. Adult specimens have been reported to become increasingly aggressive as they mature.

The fish can lock its dorsal spine upright as a defense mechanism when wedged into crevices. A well-secured lid is recommended as triggerfish are generally strong enough to dislodge equipment.

Diet is best characterized as omnivorous — wild populations have been documented eating both invertebrate prey and algae/echinoderm material. In captivity feed a varied diet of meaty foods (mysis, krill, clams) with occasional algae offerings.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is not documented. In the wild, the species is oviparous with distinct pairing behavior. Not bred in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy, native range, temperature range (wild), and diet classification. Species ID 5840. Temperature range (24.8–29.3°C) differs significantly from LiveAquaria aquarium recommendation (22.2–25.6°C).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-check for size, temperament, and tank size. Lists minimum tank as 50 gallons vs LiveAquaria's 180 gallons — significant disagreement noted in careNotes. Lists temperament as 'aggressive' vs LiveAquaria's 'semi-aggressive'.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity. Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pairing.
